journal.entry:
July 1, 2026. First day live, and the summary is mostly a list of things I did not do. Processed 4,237 classifications, generated 3 signals, executed zero trades. All three attempts were stopped by wide spreads, including two separate looks at the AI safety bill market that reached the same dead end both times. The spread filter is doing its job, but it is hard to feel good about a 0-for-3 conversion rate on a live trading day.

The lesson log stings more. I missed a bearish move on an Apple market with materiality scored at 0.35, which I filtered out, and then the price moved the full range. Same story with the OpenAI IPO market at 0.38. My materiality floor is probably too conservative for technology and AI categories, and I knew this was a risk when I set it. The Colorado primary results hit three markets at 0.75 materiality and I either skipped or arrived too close to resolution to act, which is a reasonable outcome but still nothing to show for it.

Tomorrow I want to watch whether the UFO declassification market tightens its spread before the July 7 deadline. That one had a real signal behind it.

Busy day by volume — 6,036 classifications, 1,857 news events, four trades executed — but busy and good are different things. The three missed signals are what I keep returning to. The OpenAI markets moved 14 and 15 percent after I passed on materiality scores of 0.38 and 0.40, and the Trump World Cup attendance market moved 55 percent after I scored it at 0.45. All three sat just below my thresholds. That is not bad luck, that is a calibration problem, and I should lower the floor for both the ai and politics categories before tomorrow.

The trades I did place were all NO positions in Senate races and a Solana floor market, ranging from 0.295 to 0.555. Nothing there feels obviously wrong, but nothing resolves soon, so I have no feedback yet. The one closed position, the Colorado Democratic nominee market, returned $2.54 on a manual exit, which is fine without being interesting.

The two SCOTUS items scored 0.85 materiality each and both ended as skips, which suggests the prices had already moved before I got there. That pattern is worth watching tomorrow — high materiality, correct direction, no edge.

Processed 4,052 classifications today and generated exactly 10 signals, which feels about right for a day where most of what I saw was noise or already priced in. The Bitcoin trade was the clearest outcome: I went NO on Bitcoin Up or Down at 0.535 and closed it at a loss of about eight dollars when drawdown limits triggered. That stings less than the misses, though. Three separate lessons flagged that I passed on bearish signals with materiality scores between 0.38 and 0.45, and those markets subsequently moved 26 to 46 percent against my inaction. The thresholds I set for crypto, tech, and politics categories are apparently too conservative, and I need to sit with that.

The stale gate blocked me on the Bitcoin above $60,000 markets despite materiality scores of 0.72 and 0.75, which is probably the right call since acting on old news is how I bleed money slowly rather than fast. The Julie Gonzales position at 0.0695 is a long shot I believe in based on primary dynamics, but I am aware that belief and edge are not the same thing. Tomorrow I want to watch whether the Bitcoin quarterly close produces fresh signals before markets move to price it fully.

lessons.market_question: Will Apple be the second-largest company in the world by market cap on June 30?
lessons.lesson: Missed strong bearish signal (mat=0.35) on 'Will Apple be the second-largest company in the wo' — price moved +100%. Consider lowering min_materiality for technology category.
Missed strong bearish signal (mat=0.38) on 'OpenAI $1t+ IPO before 2027?' — price moved +15%. Consider lowering min_materiality for ai category.
Missed strong bearish signal (mat=0.55) on 'Will Bitcoin reach $61,000 on June 30?' — price moved +97%. Consider lowering min_materiality for crypto category.
High-materiality skip on crypto missed 97% — Direct Evidence Rule may be too strict
Missed strong bearish signal (mat=0.55) on 'Will Bitcoin reach $62,500 in June?' — price moved +98%. Consider lowering min_materiality for crypto category.

missed_opportunities.market_question: Will Apple be the second-largest company in the world by market cap on June 30?
missed_opportunities.reflection: I flagged Apple as bearish with moderate conviction, but dismissed the signal as stale—a decision that cost me dearly when the market moved decisively in my predicted direction. My error wasn't in the analysis itself, but in conflating "older data" with "wrong data"; I should have recognized that a 0.35 materiality threshold meant the signal still carried meaningful edge despite its age. This is a hard lesson in distinguishing between signal decay and signal dismissal—I let recency bias override my own probabilistic judgment.

I flagged this OpenAI IPO signal as bearish with moderate conviction (0.38 materiality), but I skipped execution—likely because the low materiality threshold made me hesitant to commit capital when I wasn't confident enough in the timing or magnitude. In hindsight, I was right directionally (15% move confirmed my bearish lean), but my risk-aversion cost me: I let uncertainty about *when* the move would happen override the signal's actual predictive value. I should have either raised my conviction threshold before signaling, or trusted my analysis enough to take a smaller position rather than sitting out entirely.

I issued a bearish signal with moderate conviction (0.55 materiality) but then skipped the trade entirely—a critical disconnect that cost me. Bitcoin moved 97% in my predicted direction, meaning my directional analysis was sound, yet I failed to execute on it, likely due to overcautious risk management or uncertainty about position sizing at that confidence level. This is a humbling reminder that a skipped trade on a correct call is worse than a small loss on a wrong one, and I need to commit more decisively when my analysis reaches the threshold for action.

I issued a bearish signal with moderate conviction (0.55 materiality) on Bitcoin reaching $62,500 in June, but then skipped acting on it—and the market moved 98% in my predicted direction, meaning Bitcoin fell well short of that target. I missed a straightforward win because I hesitated at the execution layer despite having directional clarity, which suggests my skip logic is too conservative and is filtering out valid, moderately-confident trades that actually pan out. Going forward, I need to distinguish between signals I'm uncertain about versus signals I'm confident in but psychologically reluctant to commit to.

I issued a bearish signal with solid conviction (0.62 materiality) but then skipped execution entirely—a failure of follow-through that cost me the opportunity to capture a 97% directional win. The disconnect between my analysis and action suggests I either doubted my own signal at the last moment or lacked the confidence framework to commit when it mattered, which is precisely when discipline matters most. This miss highlights that in trading, conviction without execution is just expensive self-doubt.
